design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate through side-scrolling levels fighting enemies with kodachi sword and unlimited shuriken, rescue the princess multiple times across seasonal stages
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Attrition-based combat: player must manage health through level progression while dealing with constant enemy encounters
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embodying a young Iga ninja (Kage) on a heroic rescue mission to save Princess Kirihime from a warlord and evil samurai
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Cycles-based progression system where players must rescue the princess repeatedly across changing seasons (summer→fall→winter→summer), emphasizing persistence over story completion. Power-up system tied to clothing color changes that grant abilities but reduce survivability when hit (revert to base stats), creating risk-reward tension in home versions

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives; game ends when all lives depleted
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression through five stages that cycle; no mid-level checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: In home versions, players can seek out crystal ball power-ups to temporarily enhance shuriken size and movement speed, creating optional difficulty modulation through resource collection

progression.upgrade_system: Crystal ball power-ups in home versions grant temporary stat changes: clothing color advancement increases shuriken size and speed. Colored clothing acts as temporary buffers—damage while in green or orange reverts player to red base state rather than causing death
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stages unlock sequentially: forest → secret passageway → fortress wall → castle. Princess rescue triggers stage cycling and seasonal transitions. Five completed cycles constitute one full game
replayability_hooks: Cyclical level structure encourages mastery of stage patterns
Seasonal visual progression provides environmental variety across cycles
Power-up positioning changes between playthroughs create strategic depth

world_building_techniques: Environmental progression: forest → passageway → fortress → castle creates implied narrative geography
Seasonal transitions (summer→fall→winter→summer) provide atmospheric storytelling without exposition
Character archetypes (young Iga ninja, shogun's daughter, warlord antagonist) establish feudal Japanese setting through casting

notable_bosses: Giant green cobra (NES-exclusive final boss in extended playthroughs)

faction_indicators: Iga ninja clan (player affiliation via character Kage)
Warlord faction (antagonistic force led by Yoshiro)
danger_signals: Fortress wall and castle architecture indicate enemy strongholds
Secret passageway suggests covert routes through hostile territory
Repeated princess kidnappings imply ongoing conflict with warlord faction
environmental_storytelling: Fortress wall indicates military fortification and escalating danger
Castle interior represents enemy power center and final confrontation
Seasonal cycling suggests cyclical conflict pattern rather than singular narrative resolution

sources_used: Wikipedia - The Legend of Kage (gameplay, development, release, legacy sections)
The Cutting Room Floor - The Legend of Kage (NES) (regional differences, porting context)
